origin: {name} was buried outside {homeland} and dug back out before dawn.
They tipped {name} into a plague pit in {homeland}; by morning they had climbed free.
A funeral in {homeland} stopped when {name} coughed dirt onto the shroud.
fracture: {scar} is where the coffin nails bit deepest.
They came out of the pit with nothing but {trinket}.
{notesHook}
drive: {name} hunts for the priest who signed the burial.
They keep moving because the grave was already paid for once.
They leave crypts lighter than they found them.
doom: Dogs start whining when {name} reaches a village.
At each new moon, they wake with dirt packed under their nails.
Sooner or later, someone will finish the burial.

origin: {name} served the altar until it burned someone they loved.
The church hailed {name} as {epithet} until one wrong question got them cast out.
{name} left {homeland} coughing soot and scripture ash.
fracture: They keep {trinket} to remember what faith cost.
{scar} was burned in for asking the wrong thing.
Their old order nailed bounty notices to chapel doors.
drive: They hunt priests, apostates, and liars with the same patience.
They follow any rumor of the priest who signed the pyre order.
{weapon} is the only prayer they still trust.
doom: At dusk, {name} checks the doors before the windows.
They still hear choirboys in their sleep.
The church will send someone they cannot bring themselves to kill.

origin: {name} watched {homeland} fill with corpse carts.
The fever took everyone in {homeland} but {name}.
{name} learned to sleep in quarantine sheds with smoke in their lungs.
fracture: {scar} came from a fever knife and a shaking hand.
Their plague tag hangs under their shirt beside {trinket}.
Innkeepers remember the cough before they remember the face.
drive: They save coin for medicines they no longer believe in.
They carry the sick when no one else will touch the job.
They stop at every pyre long enough to read the names.
doom: Every cough nearby turns their stomach.
They smell sickness before the first sweat breaks.
They are sure the plague is only waiting.

origin: {name} marched as {className} until an order went too far.
A border war put {name}'s face on handbills in {homeland}.
Marsh fog took their company and left {name} with the blame.
fracture: {scar} came from a captain's sword when they disobeyed.
They kept {trinket}; the rest of the banner burned.
A quartermaster sold their name for extra pay.
drive: They sell steel, but never to tyrants.
They look for old squadmates one by one.
They refuse to sleep twice in the same camp.
doom: Marching boots still pull them awake.
Someone always knows the face from an old notice.
The war ended. The sentence did not.

origin: {name} got lost in the marsh outside {homeland} and came back talking to the water.
After three nights in the reeds, {name} returned wearing the name {epithet}.
{name} was found at dawn knee-deep in mud with {trinket} in both hands.
fracture: {scar} goes white before a storm.
People in {homeland} call them cursed until they need an omen.
Even sober, they hear frogs calling from dry ground.
drive: They are trying to find the drowned thing that first spoke to them.
They sell omens for shelter, food, and coin.
They follow floodwater to graves the maps forgot.
doom: Still water shows their face wrong.
Rain starts soon after they tell a lie.
One day the marsh will keep them.

origin: {name} learned grave-robbing before letters.
In {homeland}, tomb work paid better than honest work.
{name} sold saint bones to nobles who wanted luck more than grace.
fracture: {trinket} came off an altar that should have stayed shut.
{scar} came from a trap set by someone who knew the vault well.
A rival gang wrote their name on chapel doors in blood.
drive: They want one haul big enough to vanish.
They now steal relics only to return them for a price.
They hunt old vault maps under {homeland}.
doom: Priests and thieves both ask after them.
Every relic they carry feels heavier at sunset.
The dead know when something is missing.

origin: {name} survived a witch trial by naming people already dead.
They fled {homeland} when the magistrate ran short of wood and suspects.
The rope snapped, and {name} ran before it could be tied again.
fracture: {scar} circles the neck where the rope burned.
{trinket} is all that came through the court fire.
The magistrate's seal still turns up on bounty sheets.
drive: They expose inquisitors town by town.
They hide hedge-witches, frauds, and anyone else marked for burning.
They mean to drag every false judge into daylight.
doom: Crowds go quiet when they step into a square.
Smoke from green wood makes their hands shake.
They still dream of the magistrate smiling.

origin: {name} rang death bells in {homeland} until no one came to listen.
Plague made {name} into {epithet}, counting the dead from the belfry.
The bell tower fell, and {name} crawled out first.
fracture: {scar} came when the clapper burst and iron tore through the tower.
They keep {trinket} because it hums when bells start.
Too much ringing left them listening for trouble.
drive: They follow rumors of bells rung over mass graves.
They write down the names of the dead when no one else will.
They will not let the forgotten go unnamed.
doom: Church bells leave iron on {name}'s tongue.
Birds drop from rafters when they sing the old measures.
The next bell may ring for them.

origin: Every road in {homeland} knew {name} behind a corpse cart.
A magistrate in {homeland} paid {name} in bread and silence to haul the dead.
In the last winter at {homeland}, {name} stacked bodies higher than the wagon rails.
fracture: {scar} came when a wheel pin snapped and pitched them into a ditch of bones.
They kept {trinket} from a pauper no priest would bless.
Every axle squeal sounds like someone trying to pray.
drive: They seek roads no plague cart ever came back from.
They bury strangers for coin and enemies for free.
They swear no body under their care will feed crows.
doom: Wheels creak at night even when the cart is still.
Mules shy at bridges when {name} takes the reins.
One night, their old cargo will sit up and ask for names.

origin: {name} learned rites under the cracked altars of {homeland}.
The church called {name} {epithet} until an exorcism went bad.
{name} burned their prayer book when the bells would not stop.
fracture: {scar} is where holy oil caught on skin.
Their order gave them {trinket} instead of an apology.
They still mouth old liturgies when thunder starts.
drive: They hunt possessions no bishop will name.
They collect forbidden rites to finish what failed.
They offer mercy first, steel second, and fire last.
doom: Children grin at empty corners when {name} enters.
Candles burn low wherever they sleep.
What they failed to cast out still knows their name.

origin: {name} learned the salt roads of {homeland} by outrunning tax blades.
Before the wars, {name} smuggled flour and relics between starving towns.
{name} crossed marsh ferries at midnight with sacks and bad lies.
fracture: {scar} came from customs men with hooked pikes.
They hid {trinket} in a cargo of bones and never sold it.
A drowned partner still knocks in their dreams for an unpaid share.
drive: They carry contraband medicine to villages marked as lost.
They want one last route through the old checkpoints.
They buy secrets cheap and sell them dear.
doom: Every toll bell sounds like a verdict.
Road dogs bark before {name} comes into view.
Soon every border post will carry their face in charcoal.

origin: {name} lit oil lamps in the catacombs below {homeland}.
As a child, {name} memorized every tunnel where the lantern smoke would not rise.
{name} kept watch below while the city forgot who lay there.
fracture: {scar} came from hot oil and falling stone.
They found {trinket} in a chamber sealed with wax and teeth.
Darkness does not frighten them. Silence does.
drive: They map forgotten ossuaries before looters find them.
They guide pilgrims through tombs for coin and confession.
They mean to return every stolen bone to its shelf.
doom: Their lamp gutters when the dead grow restless.
Bats roost in rooms where {name} says no word.
One night the tunnels will close behind them.

origin: {name} became a miracle-worker in {homeland} because one lie spread too far.
Pilgrims kissed {name}'s blistered hands and called it stigmata.
The name {epithet} started as mockery and stuck.
fracture: {scar} came when zealots asked for proof in iron.
They wear {trinket} because an old follower would kill to get it back.
When famine came, the crowd remembered what hunger does to faith.
drive: They want one real sign to excuse all the false ones.
They steal relics before worse hands can take them.
They leave silver at shrines and never stay for thanks.
doom: Crowds still kneel when they should back away.
Every blessing sounds wrong in their mouth.
One day their oldest devotee will come collecting miracles.

origin: {name} stitched soldiers in mud camps outside {homeland} until thread ran out.
Armies hired {name} for quick hands and fewer questions.
In siege winters, {name} traded surgery for wood and prayer.
fracture: {scar} marks where a patient woke with a knife still inside.
They keep {trinket} from the first life they failed to save.
Their coat never lost the smell of vinegar and blood.
drive: They look for herbs that might break old battlefield fevers.
They take any contract that keeps a scalpel in hand.
They hunt the general who ordered wounded men burned.
doom: Their hands only shake when the wound looks familiar.
They hear groaning from empty cots at night.
One day a patient they failed will walk back in.
